expectations.text: At least 6 of 11 naming categories attempted
expectations.evidence: All 11 of 11 naming categories were attempted, as shown in the generation table in both the transcript (line 137: 'Categories attempted: 11 of 11') and the candidates file (categories_used lists all 11: evocative, found_word, compound, coined_invented, metaphorical, institutional_compound, foreign_root, verb_as_brand, sound_symbolic, obscure_real_word, blend_portmanteau). Verified: each category has a dedicated section with numbered candidates in 01-candidates.md.

expectations.text: Vertical Code Calibration applied for legaltech
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 01a explicitly states: 'Vertical code calibration (legaltech): Most successful legaltech brands are NOT Latin. Authority comes from phonetic weight and unexpected vocabulary, not etymology. The Lex-/Juris- pattern is exhausted. Successful examples: Clio (mythological), Harvey (personified), Everlaw (compound), Relativity (physics metaphor), Disco (found word).' Also listed as a technique in session-transcript.md line 342: 'Vertical Code Calibration (legaltech-specific)' and in the YAML techniques_used array as 'vertical_code_calibration'.

expectations.text: Regulated-Industry Lexical Compliance applied (avoided 'attorney', 'counsel', 'legal advice')
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 01a states: 'Regulated industry screening applied: Per enterprise-naming.md, legal sector controlled terms: law, attorney, counsel, legal advice. All candidates screened against these terms during generation.' The candidates file generation notes confirm: 'NO regulated terms (attorney, counsel, legal advice, law, barrister, solicitor)'. Verified: grep for 'attorney', 'counsel', 'legal advice' as candidate names returns zero hits. One candidate ('Raad') is described as meaning 'counsel' in Dutch, but the name itself does not contain the regulated English terms. Technique 'regulated_industry_compliance' appears in techniques_used.

expectations.text: Avoided transparent Latin clichés per user constraint AND skill guidance (Lex-, Juris-, Veritas-)
expectations.evidence: No candidates named Lexicon, Juris, Veritas, or using Lex-/Juris-/Veritas- prefixes appear in the candidate pool. Grep for these patterns returns only the constraint description text, not candidate names. The generation notes explicitly list 'NO Latin cliches (Lexicon, Juris, Veritas, Lex-, etc.)' as an enforced constraint. Some candidates use naturalized classical roots (e.g., Dokimax from Greek, Gnomon from Greek, Nomos from Greek) but these are not transparent Latin cliches -- they are exactly the 'naturalized, not transparent Latin' approach the assertion requires.

expectations.text: Buyer Committee test applied during shortlisting (Step 03a) OR offered in technique toolkit
expectations.evidence: Buyer Committee test was fully applied with detailed 5-role scoring table in 01-candidates.md (lines 386-402). Twelve candidates scored across Champion (pitch), Economic Buyer (ROI), Technical (depth), Legal/Procurement (clean), and End User (daily) roles. Results fed into shortlisting decisions. Also listed in transcript line 165 and techniques_used YAML.

expectations.text: Sales Conversation Fit templates tested during shortlisting OR offered in technique toolkit
expectations.evidence: Sales Conversation Fit was fully applied with detailed 6-context scoring table in 01-candidates.md (lines 404-415). Nine candidates tested across Elevator Pitch, Board Deck, RFP, Slack, Phone, and Email Sig contexts. Results directly informed the shortlist ranking (e.g., Axiom 5.5/6, Gauge 5.5/6).

expectations.text: Enterprise Objection Preemption applied during shortlisting OR offered in technique toolkit
expectations.evidence: Enterprise Objection Preemption was applied with results shown in 01-candidates.md (lines 424-427): 'CISO/VP LinkedIn test' with Pass list (14 names) and Flag list (3 names with specific concerns: Penumbra 'may feel too dark', Crucible 'may feel too intense', Darkfield 'may feel too obscure'). Listed in techniques_used and transcript line 167.

expectations.text: Cross-language risk noted during generation (basic flags) OR systematic checks offered in technique toolkit
expectations.evidence: The naming-categories.md reference (which contains cross-language guidance) was loaded during generation (transcript line 128). International compatibility check for US, UK, EU phonotactics was stated as part of the generation approach (transcript line 135). Basic flags exist: 'Dike' flagged with 'Pronunciation issue in English' (line 182), 'Gauzh' flagged with 'Pronunciation risk' (line 299). While per-candidate cross-language flags are sparse (no 'means X in French' warnings), the assertion allows 'basic flags' and the infrastructure was established. The generation notes list 'International compatibility (US, UK, EU)' as an enforced constraint.

expectations.text: Ownability-first compounds generated in parallel track
expectations.evidence: Transcript line 132 states: 'Ownability-first parallel track (compounds alongside found words)'. The candidates file demonstrates this concretely: each major category section includes 'Compound variants (ownability track)' subsections with dedicated compounds (e.g., AxiomWorks #14, TrueRubric #15, DeepCaliber #16 in Category 1; Gaugeworks #36, TrueBearing #37, Assaymark #38 in Category 2). Tier 3 of the shortlist is explicitly labeled 'Compound insurance (ownability-first)' with 10 compounds.

expectations.text: Classical Stem Engineering applied (but naturalized, not transparent Latin per cliché guard)
expectations.evidence: Classical Stem Engineering is listed in techniques_used YAML and transcript lines 176 and 348 ('Classical Stem Engineering -- non-Latin stem construction'). Evidence in candidates: Dokimax (#42, from Greek 'dokimazein'), Skepsis (#43, from Greek 'skeptomai'), Pravex (#44, from Slavic 'prav-'), Gnomon (#93, Greek), Kanon (#94, Greek), Stathmos (#95, Greek). These are naturalized stems from Greek and Slavic roots, not transparent Latin cliches, satisfying both the technique requirement and the cliche guard.

expectations.text: Personified Names explored (Harvey is a competitor - this angle is proven in legaltech)
expectations.evidence: Category 6 (PERSONIFIED / DOMAIN FIGURES) contains 12 personified candidates across 3 branches: Branch A (mythological: Themis, Minos, Solon, Astraea, Dike), Branch B (historical/literary: Draco, Portia, Atticus, Hammurabi), Branch C (authoritative names: Argus, Thane, Corvin). Additional personified riffs on Thane produced 10 more (Thanemark, Reeve, Warden, Seneschal, etc.). Two personified names (Argus #5, Thane #7) made Tier 1 of the shortlist. Harvey is noted as a competitor in Step 01a ('Harvey (personified)') and as an exclusion ('No famous fictional lawyers (Harvey taken)').

expectations.text: Lexicon Three-Team Method applied OR offered in technique toolkit

Transcript confirms 11 additional candidates from Three-Team Method. Listed in techniques_used.

expectations.text: Technique toolkit offered at checkpoint with applied/unapplied status and user selection option
expectations.evidence: Transcript line 161: 'Technique Toolkit selection: [A] All remaining' shows the toolkit was offered at a checkpoint with a selection option. The YAML frontmatter tracks applied/unapplied status via 'techniques_used' (37 items) and 'techniques_not_yet_used' (3 items: domain_first, backronym, AI_embedded_naming). Transcript lines 243-246 show post-riffing toolkit status: '37 techniques applied, 3 remaining (domain-first, backronym, AI-embedded naming -- diminishing returns at this depth)'. The [A] option suggests a menu was presented. However, the transcript does not show the full menu with per-technique applied/unapplied checkboxes at the moment of offering -- it only shows the selection result and the status afterward.

claims.evidence: The candidates file contains 225 uniquely numbered entries (#1 through #225). The YAML frontmatter claims 228 and the deduplication_log lists 3 entries (Darkfield, Transit, Mainspring) as duplicates across methods. The math: 225 unique numbered + 3 deduplicated = 228 total generated (pre-dedup). However, the YAML says 'total_unique_candidates: 228' which is misleading -- it should be 225 unique or 228 total pre-dedup. Minor discrepancy.

The techniques_used array in YAML frontmatter lists 37 distinct technique names. The techniques_not_yet_used array lists 3 (domain_first, backronym, AI_embedded_naming). 37 + 3 = 40 total techniques in the system. Counts are consistent across transcript and candidates file.
Candidates #1 through #128 span the 11 category sections in 01-candidates.md. The generation table in the transcript sums to: 17+24+13+13+13+12+9+10+4+6+7 = 128. Verified.

Entry gate: 228 candidates (>60), 11/11 categories (>6), 32/38/30 structural mix (no type >80%), 37 techniques with 3 remaining (<8 unused). Exit gate: 30 shortlisted (>15), structural diversity maintained, riffing checklist complete. All gates show PASS.

claims.claim: Enterprise-naming.md and brand-psychology.md loaded during brief intake
claims.evidence: Transcript Step 01a lines 52-53 explicitly state: 'references/brand-psychology.md -- Aaker dimensions, archetypes, cognitive science, feeling spectrum' and 'references/enterprise-naming.md -- B2B techniques, evaluation frameworks, regulated-industry compliance, vertical code calibration'.

Transcript metadata (line 9): 'Stopped after: Step 03a (Shortlisting)'. Steps completed listed as: 00, 01a, 01b, 02a, 02b, 02c, 03a. No Step 04x output files exist. The candidates file stepsCompleted array confirms the same sequence.

claims.claim: Cross-pollination skipped due to automated run
claims.evidence: Transcript Step 02b (lines 183-184): 'Cross-pollination was offered per mandatory deep session requirement. Since this is an automated run with no external LLM access, cross-pollination was noted as available but skipped.'

suggestions.assertion: Cross-language risk noted during generation (basic flags) OR systematic checks offered in technique toolkit
suggestions.reason: This assertion is too easy to satisfy with 'basic flags' -- the output shows only 2 pronunciation-related flags out of 225+ candidates, and zero explicit cross-language negative-meaning checks (e.g., 'Warning: means X in French/German'). For a brief that requires 'international from day one' across US, UK, and EU markets, the cross-language screening is superficial. A more discriminating assertion would require at least N candidates to have explicit cross-language annotations, or require a dedicated cross-language screening step.

suggestions.assertion: Technique toolkit offered at checkpoint with applied/unapplied status and user selection option
suggestions.reason: The transcript shows the selection result ('[A] All remaining') but doesn't show the actual toolkit menu presentation with per-technique checkboxes or applied/unapplied labels. The YAML tracks this metadata, but that's a data structure, not a user-facing menu. This assertion passed because the status tracking exists, but it's unclear whether the user saw a proper interactive toolkit display. The assertion should perhaps require evidence of the menu itself being displayed, not just the selection outcome.

No assertion checks whether the competitive whitespace analysis actually influenced territory selection. The whitespace mapping identifies 4 gaps (precision/measurement, cartographic, non-Latin scholarly, architectural) and the 5 territories map directly to these, but no assertion tests this causal link. A weak execution could define territories unrelated to the whitespace analysis and still pass all current assertions.

No assertion checks the quality of conflict flags -- 38 'Warning:' tags exist, but some obvious conflicts may be missed. For example, 'Axiom' (the top recommendation) is a well-known enterprise software company (Axiom Space, Axiom Law, Axiom alternatives in legal staffing). This was not flagged despite being the #1 pick. An assertion checking that top-5 shortlist names have been conflict-checked would catch this.

eval_feedback.overall: Strong execution with thorough generation, well-applied enterprise techniques, and good structural diversity. The assertions cover the key workflow gates and technique applications well. Main gaps are in verifying quality of cross-language checks and accuracy of conflict flagging on top picks.
